---
title: Maryland Drinking Water Quality Violations ETL
author: Connor Hall,
subtitle: "Final project for INST447, Spring 2026"
format: html
---

# Executive Summary

The text in this section includes:

- overview summary
- discussion of audience & purpose
- conclusions/final discussion


# Source(s) & Extraction

## Data Source
The data sources can be downloaded as a .zip from https://echo.epa.gov/tools/data-downloads#downloads
under the "Drinking Water Data Downloads" section.

## Data Generation and Collection

The data was generated when contaminants entered drinking water. The data is collected by any entity required by the
Safe Water Drinking Act to enforce drinking water regulations. Each entity reports their data to the EPA, which is
then stored in the SDWIS Fed Data Warehouse.

Source: https://www.epa.gov/ground-water-and-drinking-water/safe-drinking-water-information-system-sdwis-federal-reporting

## Original Variables

### SDWA_VIOLATIONS_ENFORCEMENT.csv
- PWSID: Unique code for a public water system. Consists of a two-letter state or region code and 7 digits.
- VIOLATION_ID: Identifier for a violation.
- FACILITY_ID: Identifier for a facility in a public water system.
- NON_COMPL_PER_BEGIN_DATE: Date when violation has been identified.
- NON_COMPL_PER_END_DATE: Date when violation has ended. Ongoing violations have a null value.
- CONTAMINANT_CODE: Code representing a contaminant.
- VIOL_MEASURE: The amount of the contaminant.
- UNIT_OF_MEASURE: The unit of measure.

### SDWA_FACILITIES.csv
- PWSID: Unique code for a public water system. Consists of a two-letter state or region code and 7 digits.
- FACILITY_ID: Identifier for a facility in a public water system.
- FACILITY_NAME: The name of the facility.
- FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE: A single-letter code identifying the facility's operating status.
- FACILITY_TYPE_CODE: A two-letter code identifying the type of facility.
- WATER_TYPE_CODE: A two-letter code identifying the water source.

### SDWA_GEOGRAPHIC_AREAS.csv
- PWSID: Unique code for a public water system. Consists of a two-letter state or region code and 7 digits.
- COUNTY_SERVED: The county served by the public water system.

## Shrinking Data
The original data is large and must be shrunk down. **Do not run this code**. It has already been run and the datasets it created have been provided in the repository.
The code is here to demonstrate how the data was shrunk.

Each dataset was loaded using read_csv(). The columns of interest were selected with the usecols parameter. The datatype of the FACILITY_ID column in the violations dataframe
was cast as a string. This will be important later. The violations dataframe's null values and duplicate rows were dropped to shrink the dataframe further.

The PWSID and FACILITY_ID columns uniquely identify public water system facilities. To shrink the facilities and locations dataframes, a list of unique PWSID and FACILITY_ID values were created
from the violations dataframe. The PWSID and FACILITY_ID columns in the facilities and locations dataframes are strings, so the unique values must also be strings. The dataframes were filtered by selected rows 
where their values are present in the unique values list.

The dataframes were exported as csv files.

```{python}
violations = pd.read_csv('SDWA_VIOLATIONS_ENFORCEMENT.csv',
                         usecols=['PWSID', 'VIOLATION_ID', 'FACILITY_ID', 'NON_COMPL_PER_BEGIN_DATE', 'NON_COMPL_PER_END_DATE',
                                  'CONTAMINANT_CODE', 'VIOL_MEASURE', 'UNIT_OF_MEASURE'],
                         dtype={'FACILITY_ID': 'str'}).dropna().drop_duplicates()


# get unique PWSID and FACILITY_ID
sys_id = violations['PWSID'].unique().tolist()
fac_id = violations['FACILITY_ID'].unique().tolist()


facilities = pd.read_csv('SDWA_FACILITIES.csv',
                         usecols=['PWSID', 'FACILITY_ID', 'FACILITY_NAME',
                                  'FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE', 'FACILITY_TYPE_CODE',
                                  'WATER_TYPE_CODE'])
# select rows where PWSID and FACILITY_ID are in violations
facilities = facilities[facilities['PWSID'].isin(sys_id) & facilities['FACILITY_ID'].isin(fac_id)]


locations = pd.read_csv('SDWA_GEOGRAPHIC_AREAS.csv',
                        usecols=['PWSID', 'COUNTY_SERVED'])
# select rows where PWSID are in violations
locations = locations[locations['PWSID'].isin(sys_id)]


facilities.to_csv('facilities.csv', index=False)
locations.to_csv('locations.csv', index=False)
violations.to_csv('violations.csv', index=False)
```

## Extracting & Merging
Each CSV was loaded using read_csv(). The datatype of each dataframe's columns were printed to identify columns with incorrect datatypes. Next, the unique values of columns that
can be represented categorically were printed to verify that they had a fixed set of values. Datatypes were then changed.

The CONTAMINANT_CODE column in the violations dataframe required additional transformations. This column was formatted as a decimal, but the same column in the contaminant_codes
dataframe was formatted as a string. To resolve this discrepancy, the violation dataframe's column was cast as a string and the decimal point and following values were replaced with a blank string
by a regular expression. This method was necessary because some values begin with "0" and converting the column to an integer would remove the "0", therefore altering the contaminant code.

Before merging the dataframes, the contaminant_codes dataframe was filtered. This dataframe contains other reference codes, which were filtered out by selecting rows
where the VALUE_TYPE column was equal to "CONTAMINANT_CODE". The VALUE_CODE and VALUE_DESCRIPTION columns were then selected.

The dataframes were then merged. The facilities and locations dataframes were inner merged on the PWSID column. The resulting dataframe, merged_df, was inner merged with the violations dataframe
on the PWSID and FACILITY_ID columns. Lastly, merged_df was left merged with the contaminant_codes dataframe; the left dataframe (merged_df) merged on the "CONTAMINANT_CODE" column and the right dataframe (contaminant_codes) merged on the "VALUE_CODE" column.

```{python}
import pandas as pd

facilities = pd.read_csv('data/facilities.csv')
locations = pd.read_csv('data/locations.csv')
# set dtype to resolve mixed type warning
violations = pd.read_csv('data/violations.csv', dtype={'VIOLATION_ID': 'str'})
# set dtype to resolve mixed type warning
contaminant_codes = pd.read_csv('data/SDWA_REF_CODE_VALUES.csv', dtype={'VALUE_CODE': 'str'})

# output each dataframe's data types 
print(f'facilities:\n{facilities.dtypes}\n\nlocations:\n{locations.dtypes}\n\nviolations:\n{violations.dtypes}\n\ncodes:\n{contaminant_codes.dtypes}\n')

# view unique values to determine whether category is proper dtype- need small set of fixed values
print(f'FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE: {facilities['FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE'].unique()}')
print(f'FACILITY_TYPE_CODE: {facilities['FACILITY_TYPE_CODE'].unique()}')
print(f'WATER_TYPE_CODE: {facilities['WATER_TYPE_CODE'].unique()}')
print(f'CONTAMINANT_CODE: {violations['CONTAMINANT_CODE'].unique()}')
print(f'UNIT_OF_MEASURE: {violations['UNIT_OF_MEASURE'].unique()}')
print('\n')

# change dtypes
facilities = facilities.astype(dtype={'FACILITY_ID': 'str',
                                'FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE': 'category',
                                'FACILITY_TYPE_CODE': 'category',
                                'WATER_TYPE_CODE': 'category'})

violations = violations.astype(dtype={'FACILITY_ID': 'str',                   
                                'NON_COMPL_PER_BEGIN_DATE': 'datetime64[ms]',
                                'NON_COMPL_PER_END_DATE': 'datetime64[ms]',
                                'CONTAMINANT_CODE': 'str',
                                'UNIT_OF_MEASURE': 'category'})
# remove decimal from CONTAMINANT_CODE                              
violations['CONTAMINANT_CODE'] = violations['CONTAMINANT_CODE'].str.replace('\.\d+', '', regex=True)                               

# get only contaminant codes
contaminant_codes = contaminant_codes[contaminant_codes['VALUE_TYPE'] == 'CONTAMINANT_CODE'][['VALUE_CODE', 'VALUE_DESCRIPTION']]

# merge dataframes
merged_df = pd.merge(facilities, locations, on='PWSID')
merged_df = pd.merge(merged_df, violations, on=['PWSID', 'FACILITY_ID'])
merged_df = pd.merge(merged_df, contaminant_codes, how='left', left_on='CONTAMINANT_CODE', right_on='VALUE_CODE')
print(merged_df)
```

# Transformation Processes

PWSID is formatted as 2 letters followed by 7 digits. Rows that didn't follow this format were removed by a regular expression that matched
2 A-Z characters followed by 7 digits from the beginning of the string.

Outliers in the VIOL_MEASURE column were found by finding its minimum and maximum values. The maximum value was 99,999, so the values were investigated further by printing values
larger than 1000. The resulting set returned a variety of measurements, but 99,999 was a repeated value that had a null VALUE_CODE and VALUE_DESCRIPTION. Rows with this value were removed
because they are likely incorrect.

The VALUE_CODE column, which originated from the contaminant_codes dataframe, was removed because it is a duplicate of the CONTAMINANT_CODE column.

The columns were reordered and renamed. They were reordered to place similar columns together, such as FACILITY_ID and FACILITY_NAME. The column names were converted to lowercase and
some were shortened or simplified to make the column's purpose clearer. facility_activity_code in this data is equal to either A (active) or I (inactive), so it was changed to "facility_status".
non_compl_per_begin_date and non_compl_per_end_date were shortened to "begin_date" and "end_date" respectively. value_description was changed to "contaminant_name" because the values in this
column are names, not descriptions. viol_measure was changed to "measure". unit_of_measure was changed to "unit" because its values and position next to the measurement column allow its meaning to be assumed.

```{python}
# PWSID must be 2 letter state/region code and 7 digits
merged_df = merged_df[merged_df['PWSID'].str.match('[A-Z]{2}\d{7}')]

# find outliers
print(merged_df['VIOL_MEASURE'].describe())
print(merged_df[merged_df['VIOL_MEASURE'] > 1000])
# remove outliers
merged_df = merged_df[merged_df['VIOL_MEASURE'] < 99999]

# remove VALUE_CODE column- redundant
merged_df = merged_df.drop(['VALUE_CODE'], axis=1)

# reorder columns
merged_df = merged_df[['PWSID', 'VIOLATION_ID', 'FACILITY_ID', 'FACILITY_NAME', 'COUNTY_SERVED', 'FACILITY_ACTIVITY_CODE',
              'FACILITY_TYPE_CODE', 'WATER_TYPE_CODE', 'NON_COMPL_PER_BEGIN_DATE', 'NON_COMPL_PER_END_DATE',
              'CONTAMINANT_CODE', 'VALUE_DESCRIPTION', 'VIOL_MEASURE', 'UNIT_OF_MEASURE']]

# rename columns
merged_df.columns = merged_df.columns.str.lower()  
merged_df = merged_df.rename(columns={'facility_activity_code': 'facility_status',
                              'non_compl_per_begin_date': 'begin_date',
                              'non_compl_per_end_date': 'end_date',
                              'value_description': 'contaminant_name',
                              'viol_measure': 'measure',
                              'unit_of_measure': 'unit'})
                            
print(merged_df)                              
```

# Analysis & Evaluation

The text in this section includes:

- descriptions of all variables
- noteworthy patterns,
- including discussion of potential sources of bias or unbalanced patterns

You are encouraged to structure this major section into subsections as you wish, and to break up and interweave code chunks, to help with organization and readability.

```{python}
# The code chunk(s) in this section should include:
# - descriptive stats for all relevant variables
# - descriptive stat(s) for any relevant relationships
# - visualizations for all relevant variables
# - visualizations for any relevant relationships
# - code for exploring potential sources of bias, including missing data, unbalanced groups, etc.


```


# Data Product & Guide

The text in this section includes:

- description and "user's guide" for the final data product
- general info and description on each variable/column in the data product
  - note: the descriptive stats, etc. should already be in the Analysis section, but if there are any new/transformed variables that were not in that section, those should have descriptive stats and plots here

```{python}
# The code chunk(s) in this section should include:
# - The code needed to "finalize" or "export" the final data product.

```
